metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit RED test cases for AC3: (a) a unit test asserting `repair_json` is called with `schema=<model-json-schema>` and `schema_repair_mode='strict'` (using a mock/spy on the json-repair call site in structured.py:161), with a fallback-on-exception assertion; (b) a unit test asserting the reask prompt sent at runner.py:530 contains the bounded prior output snippet (≤2000 chars) and the ellipsis marker when the output exceeds the limit — these tests should be RED before the implementation and GREEN after.
findings.finding: AC3 ('json_repair called with schema=<model>; reask includes bounded faulty prior output') has no described RED test cases to flip GREEN. The plan's stated proving command for these additions is subsumed under 'make test / RED silent-success cases flip GREEN' (AC5), but arawana's RED baseline covers silent-success detection (truncation/refusal finish_reason), NOT the two structured.py self-heal changes — which are described as 'confirmed absent today' but have no named test asserting the new call signatures. An executor could add the schema= arg and the faulty-output snippet and ship passing CI without a single assertion ever exercising the new behavior.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; also 'Output self-heal in src/rebar/llm/structured.py' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's AC3, replace 'json_repair is called with schema=<model>; the targeted reask includes the bounded faulty prior output' with: 'A unit test using unittest.mock.patch on json_repair asserts it is called with schema=<verdict-model-json-schema> and schema_repair_mode="strict"; a separate unit test capturing the reask prompt string asserts it contains str(result.output)[:2000] and ends with an ellipsis marker when output exceeds 2000 chars; both tests are RED on the current codebase and GREEN after the change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move 'A follow-on ticket is filed to characterize the live Anthropic refusal-as-content signal' out of AC1 into a separate non-gating action item (e.g. a 'Follow-on actions' section), and add a concrete verification step such as a PR description checklist entry or a linked ticket URL — so the code ACs in AC1 are verifiable by `make test` alone.
findings.finding: AC1 bundles 'follow-on ticket is filed' as a completion criterion alongside three code-observable sub-claims (truncation routing, NativeOutput gap closed, refusal-as-content probe). Filing a ticket is an administrative action with no repo artifact or deterministic command to verify it — it cannot be evaluated by CI or any `make` target, violating E6's requirement that every completion-relevant claim has a concrete proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, last sentence: 'A follow-on ticket is filed to characterize the live Anthropic refusal-as-content signal.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pyproject.toml` to the plan's 'Files' section so the dep-pin bump (`json-repair>=0.30` → `>=0.61`) is an enumerated, reviewable edit target.
findings.finding: The plan names `pyproject.toml` as an edit target (dep pin bump `json-repair>=0.30` → `>=0.61`) but does not enumerate it in the plan's 'Files' section. The Files section lists only `src/rebar/llm/structured.py`, `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`, and `src/rebar/schemas/*`. `pyproject.toml` is outside `src/rebar/llm/` and is the shared package manifest — a missed edit target in the enumeration.
findings.location: Files section; 'Output self-heal' paragraph ('this story BUMPS the pin to `json-repair>=0.61`')
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Files line to: '`src/rebar/llm/structured.py`, `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`, `pyproject.toml` (json-repair pin bump), read-only on `src/rebar/schemas/*`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (a) the semantic of `schema_repair_mode='strict'` (does it raise on unresolvable schema violations, and if so under what condition), and (b) which exception type(s) the fallback try/except should catch — either cite the json-repair exception class name confirmed by the offline experiment, or bound the catch to `Exception` with an explicit comment explaining the breadth is intentional.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `schema_repair_mode='strict'` for the schema-guided repair_json call but never states what 'strict' means semantically (raise on unresolvable schema violations vs. coerce more aggressively), nor specifies which exception type(s) the fallback try/except should catch. json-repair's exception taxonomy is not documented in the plan or the experiment log. An implementer writing `except Exception` as the fallback catch is too broad (masks bugs); catching a named exception from json_repair requires knowing that json-repair raises a specific type on a schema-guided failure vs. a parse failure — neither of which the plan specifies.
findings.location: What section, Output self-heal paragraph (1): 'wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (and test) for the schema-guided repair fallback: assert that when `repair_json(..., schema=..., schema_repair_mode='strict')` raises, the fallback to schema-less `repair_json` is invoked AND a warning/metric is emitted (so the degradation is not silent).
findings.finding: The schema-guided json_repair fallback branch (try/except that reverts to schema-less repair_json when the schema-guided call raises) introduces a new failure path with no explicit test called out in the ACs — a malformed schema arg or json-repair edge case could silently degrade to the old behavior without any observable signal.
findings.location: AC line 3 / structured.py:161 delta / 'wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 3: 'The schema-guided repair fallback (try/except) is exercised by a test that injects a schema-guided repair failure and asserts (a) the schema-less repair path runs and (b) a structured warning/log entry is produced so the fallback is observable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the 'A follow-on ticket is filed' AC clause with a verifiable proxy — e.g., a `# TODO(drake): <ticket-id>` comment added to the `provider_details` probe helper, checked by a lint rule or grep in CI, so ticket creation has a code-traceable artifact.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria include 'A follow-on ticket is filed to characterize the live Anthropic refusal-as-content signal' as a deliverable, but filing a ticket is a process artifact — not testable by `make test`, `make lint`, or `make typecheck`. This means one named AC goal has no code-observable, automated proof of completion, violating the E3 requirement that each non-deferred goal have a faithful, end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '...A follow-on ticket is filed to characterize the live Anthropic refusal-as-content signal.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, change 'A follow-on ticket is filed' to 'A `# TODO(drake-followon): characterize live Anthropic refusal-as-content provider_details shape` comment is present in the probe helper, and the ticket URL is recorded in `docs/decisions/drake-followon.md`' — both checkable by grep/CI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact call-site (and in which file/function) where `.outcome = LLMOutcome(resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, ...)` is attached to the newly raised `UnretryableOutputError` — either at the raise-site in the new helper or at an intercept in `runner.py`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a detected refusal-as-content raises `UnretryableOutputError` with an `.outcome` attribute attached (built directly as `LLMOutcome(resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, diagnostic=sanitize_diagnostic({...}), retryable=False)`), but never names the code location where this `.outcome` is attached to the raised exception. The existing `.outcome` attachment only occurs at `runner.py:389` inside the generic `except Exception` seam — not in the structured path. The new `UnretryableOutputError` raised from the NativeOutput branch would escape through `runner.py:369` (`except LLMError: raise`) WITHOUT `.outcome` being set, unless the plan's new code explicitly attaches it at raise-time.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' paragraph; `runner.py:369` and `runner.py:389`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'Routing mechanism' paragraph: 'The new helper that raises `UnretryableOutputError` for the NativeOutput path attaches `.outcome` directly at the raise site: `err = UnretryableOutputError(...); err.outcome = LLMOutcome(...); raise err`.' This mirrors how `runner.py:382-389` attaches `.outcome` to `LLMUnavailableError`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact fields (at minimum `finish_reason`, `message`, `exception_type`) to populate in the `sanitize_diagnostic({...})` dict for the refusal-as-content `LLMOutcome` construction.
findings.finding: The diagnostic field content for the directly-constructed `LLMOutcome` (the refusal-as-content raise path) is never specified. The plan says to call `LLMOutcome(resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, diagnostic=sanitize_diagnostic({...}), retryable=False)` but never enumerates what keys the `{...}` dict should contain. `sanitize_diagnostic` allowlists exactly 12 named fields (`exception_type`, `status_code`, `error_code`, `error_type`, `finish_reason`, `retry_after`, `attempts`, `trace_id`, `model`, `provider`, `request_limit`, `tool_calls_limit`, `message`) — an executor writing this code must know which of those to populate for a refusal-as-content detection, but the plan provides no specification.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' sub-bullet: 'LLMOutcome(resolution_class=CHANGE_INPUT, diagnostic=sanitize_diagnostic({...}), retryable=False)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `{...}` with `{'finish_reason': finish_reason, 'message': 'refusal-as-content detected via provider_details', 'exception_type': 'UnretryableOutputError'}` (mirroring the fields `_base_diagnostic` populates from the structured seam).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact code location where `.outcome` is attached to the new `UnretryableOutputError` (e.g. immediately before the raise inside the NativeOutput branch of `_pai_structured`, or by modifying `runner.py`'s `except LLMError: raise` to attach `.outcome` for `UnretryableOutputError` instances).
findings.finding: The `.outcome` attachment placement on the new `UnretryableOutputError` raised by the NativeOutput path is ambiguous. The plan says the raised error 'carries an `LLMOutcome` built DIRECTLY ... attached as `.outcome`' but never specifies WHERE this attachment occurs — inside the new NativeOutput branch of `_pai_structured`, in the `runner.py` outer try/except, or in a new helper. The existing codebase attaches `.outcome` only once (runner.py:389 on `LLMUnavailableError` in the outer `except Exception` block), but `UnretryableOutputError` is re-raised via the `except LLMError: raise` passthrough (runner.py:368-369), bypassing the attachment site entirely.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' sub-bullet; runner.py:368-369 (except LLMError: raise), runner.py:389 (only .outcome attachment site)
findings.suggested_fix: Add the .outcome attachment directly in the new NativeOutput branch before the raise: `err = UnretryableOutputError(...); err.outcome = LLMOutcome(...); raise err` — consistent with how the plan describes it and avoids touching the outer except flow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which exception type(s) the `try/except` around `repair_json(cand, schema=..., schema_repair_mode='strict')` should catch (e.g. `ValueError`, `TypeError`, or a `json_repair`-specific exception), and clarify whether 'strict' mode raises on schema mismatch or returns a degraded value.
findings.finding: The `schema_repair_mode` parameter's value vocabulary and failure semantics are never defined. The plan specifies `schema_repair_mode='strict'` as the value to pass, but does not define what 'strict' mode does versus other legal values, what conditions cause the schema-guided call to raise (schema-validation failure? malformed schema? type error?), or whether the fallback try/except should distinguish a schema-mismatch raise (expected, worth catching) from a programming error (unexpected, should propagate). The plan's experiment log confirms the parameter exists but does not characterize its behavior.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1): 'schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode=\'strict\'' and 'wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC1 into three separate, independently verifiable ACs: AC1a (truncation-in-a-valid-verdict routed INDETERMINATE, proved by the existing arawana RED test flipping GREEN); AC1b (NativeOutput path invokes check_stop_reason, proved by a unit test against the NativeOutput branch); AC1c (refusal-as-content probe fires on canned structured marker and NO-OPs on normal verdict, proved by two distinct assertions).
findings.finding: AC1 bundles four distinct sub-outcomes into a single acceptance criterion — (a) truncation-in-a-valid-verdict routed as INDETERMINATE, (b) NativeOutput path runs check_stop_reason, (c) refusal-as-content probe fires on structured marker and NO-OPs otherwise, (d) follow-on ticket filed — making it impossible to evaluate as a single observable outcome and creating ambiguity about which subset must be true for AC1 to pass (F1: coherent single-outcome deliverable).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1

findings.finding: The Anthropic-specific `provider_details` key path for refusal-as-content detection is never specified. The plan says `provider_details` 'may carry the raw stop_reason' for Anthropic but names no concrete key to read — in contrast to the OpenAI path, which is fully specified as `provider_details.get('incomplete_details',{}).get('reason') == 'content_filter'`. An implementer cannot write the Anthropic detection branch without knowing which key(s) to inspect.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol (concrete, distinct from check_stop_reason)': "for Anthropic (rebar's provider), `getattr(result.response,'finish_reason',None)` normalizes a refusal to `content_filter` and `provider_details` (a dict, present on `ModelResponse`) may carry the raw `stop_reason`"

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback step for the json-repair pin bump (e.g., 'to revert: restore `json-repair>=0.30` in pyproject.toml and remove the `schema=`/`schema_repair_mode=` arguments from the `repair_json` call, relying on the try/except fallback path') so the change is reversible without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The json-repair version bump (pyproject.toml: >=0.30 → >=0.61) is acknowledged and justified but no rollback or expand-contract path is described. Per T4, acknowledgement alone is insufficient — the plan must describe a reversibility mechanism (e.g., 'revert pin to >=0.30 and remove the schema= call' as a named back-out step) in case the bumped version introduces a regression in environments where only 0.30–0.60 is available.
findings.location: What section — 'Since the current pyproject.toml pins json-repair>=0.30 … this story BUMPS the pin to json-repair>=0.61'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback' sub-section under the json-repair delta: 'If the >=0.61 pin causes environment or behavioral regressions, revert pyproject.toml to `json-repair>=0.30` and remove the `schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode=\'strict\'` kwargs; the try/except already falls back to schema-less repair, so the revert is a one-commit change with no data loss.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #3 to explicitly require and verify the try/except fallback: e.g., 'the schema-guided `repair_json` call is wrapped in a try/except that falls back to schema-less `repair_json`; a test confirms that a pathological input causing the schema-guided call to raise does not propagate the exception to the caller.'
findings.finding: AC #3 ('json_repair is called with schema=<model>') does not cover the try/except fallback behavior described in the body as critical for correctness ('falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises — so a json-repair edge case never regresses today's behavior'). An implementer reading only the ACs could omit the fallback and satisfy the criterion literally while breaking the regression guarantee.
findings.location: AC #3 vs. What section — 'wrapped in a try/except that falls back to the current schema-less repair_json if the schema-guided call raises'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC #3 (or as a separate sub-criterion): 'pyproject.toml `json-repair` lower bound is updated to `>=0.61`, verified by inspecting the file in the PR diff.'
findings.finding: The pyproject.toml pin bump to `json-repair>=0.61` is a concrete file-change deliverable described in the body, but no AC explicitly verifies it. AC #3 could be satisfied by an environment that already has >=0.61 without any pyproject.toml change, leaving the pin bump untracked and potentially unreviewable.
findings.location: AC #3 vs. What section — 'this story BUMPS the pin to json-repair>=0.61'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one structured log line (e.g., `logger.warning('silent_success_detected', kind=..., finish_reason=...)`) in each new detection branch so operators can observe detection rate and debug false-positives/negatives.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation (log line, metric, or trace) is planned for the new silent-success detection events (truncation-in-a-valid-verdict and refusal-as-content). Operators have no visibility into how often these conditions fire in production.
findings.location: AC line 1 / 'What' section — truncation-conjunction and refusal-as-content routing logic
findings.suggested_fix: In each new detection branch (truncation-conjunction and refusal-as-content), emit a `logger.warning` with structured fields (`kind`, `finish_reason`, `provider_details_marker`, `model_name`) before raising `UnretryableOutputError`. Optionally emit a counter metric via the existing telemetry hook if one exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section (or inline module docstring block in structured.py/runner.py) documenting: (a) why provider_details is probed and which keys are trusted per-provider, (b) why UnretryableOutputError is raised directly rather than routed through classify_llm_failure, and (c) the follow-on ticket reference for Anthropic refusal-as-content characterization.
findings.finding: No ADR or design-doc update is planned for the novel silent-success detection pattern, the provider_details probe contract, or the routing decision (refusal-as-content → INDETERMINATE via UnretryableOutputError rather than through classify_llm_failure). Future maintainers have no durable record of why provider_details is probed, which keys are trusted, or why the direct LLMOutcome construction path was chosen over the exception-classifier.
findings.location: 'What' section — Refusal-as-content protocol; Routing mechanism paragraphs; 'Files' list (no ADR/CLAUDE.md entry)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement note or follow-on ticket to track reask success rate before/after the snippet addition (even a simple counter of 'reask succeeded after snippet' vs 'reask failed') so _FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS can be tuned empirically.
findings.finding: The `_FAULTY_OUTPUT_SNIPPET_CHARS = 2000` threshold for the faulty-prior-output snippet in the reask prompt is asserted without any measurement or reference to empirical data showing it is effective. While it is a named constant (good), there is no measurement plan or pilot metric to validate that 2000 chars improves reask success rates vs. a smaller or larger value.
findings.location: 'What' section — Output self-heal delta (2): runner.py:530 reask snippet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact `provider_details` key path pydantic-ai exposes for an OpenAI content-filter response (verify against pydantic-ai source or `--help`/docs, not inference) and add a canned-response test that asserts the helper fires on that exact shape before shipping the OpenAI probe.
findings.finding: The plan's secondary refusal-as-content probe for OpenAI keys on `provider_details['incomplete_details']['reason'] == 'content_filter'` (a specific OpenAI API response field path), but no artifact in the repo independently confirms this key path exists in pydantic-ai's `ModelResponse.provider_details` for an OpenAI content-filter response. A search for 'incomplete_details' finds only the plan-review cache files, not production code or tests. If the key path is wrong or absent (e.g. pydantic-ai normalizes it differently), the probe silently NO-OPs — which is the designed fail-open behavior — but the implementation code would reference a dead path with no test to catch the drift.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol (concrete)': 'incomplete_details.reason == content_filter for OpenAI'
findings.suggested_fix: Check `pydantic_ai/models/openai.py` in the installed pydantic-ai package for how `provider_details` is populated on a content-filtered response; update the helper to use the confirmed key (could be `finish_details`, `content_filter_results`, or `incomplete_details` — verify empirically). Record the confirmed path in the plan's experiment log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete offline test (no model call) that calls `repair_json` with `schema=<a small test model_json_schema dict>` and `schema_repair_mode='strict'` and asserts the repaired output matches the schema — this simultaneously confirms the param names/types and proves AC3 with a RED→GREEN flip.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json` in json-repair >=0.61.2 accepts `schema=` and `schema_repair_mode=` parameters ('VERIFIED offline'), but the only evidence is the plan's own self-reported experiment log (a parameter name listing). No independently observable artifact in the repo (no pinned version bump yet, no changelog cite, no import of these params anywhere in the codebase, no test using them) confirms the claim. The current pin `json-repair>=0.30` predates these params; the story correctly identifies the need to bump to `>=0.61` but that bump is itself contingent on the params existing as described.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py (1)': 'add schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode=strict'; Verified library behavior section: 'json-repair 0.61.2: repair_json params = [...] schema + schema_repair_mode present'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC3 test, directly call `from json_repair import repair_json; repair_json('{"x": 1,}', return_objects=True, schema={"type":"object","properties":{"x":{"type":"integer"}}}, schema_repair_mode='strict')` and assert a non-empty result. If this raises TypeError, the param name is wrong and the plan needs correction before the version bump.

findings.finding: The plan's Anthropic refusal-as-content detection rule asserts that `ModelResponse.provider_details` (described as 'a dict, present on ModelResponse') 'may carry the raw stop_reason' for out-of-band Anthropic filter signals. No source file in the repo reads `provider_details` from any ModelResponse, and no test exercises this field. The entire premise rests solely on the plan's own 'Verified library behavior' prose block — no repo artifact (fixture, lockfile, test data, import trace) grounds it.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol' paragraph; 'Verified library behavior' block
findings.suggested_fix: Add a canned-response test (alongside the existing canned `content_filter`/`length` tests in `test_llm_failure_matrix.py`) that constructs a mock `ModelResponse` with non-empty text parts and a `provider_details` dict in the expected shape, and asserts the refusal-as-content detector fires. This both proves the field exists in the expected form and pins the contract.

findings.finding: The plan's refusal-as-content detection for the OpenAI path keys on `provider_details.get('incomplete_details',{}).get('reason') == 'content_filter'`, but `provider_details` on pydantic-ai's `ModelResponse` is listed only in the inline experiment log (no doc link, no code cite, no independent artifact). The specific nested key path `incomplete_details.reason` is Unverified — the experiment log shows the field exists but does not show the dict structure for either the Anthropic or OpenAI provider. If the key path is wrong (e.g. the actual key is `'finish_details'` or structurally different), the refusal-as-content gate silently never fires, which is a correctness failure precisely for the case the plan is trying to close.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation, add a debug-log that dumps `provider_details` on any non-`stop` finish reason during the detection check. Write a test fixture that constructs a `ModelResponse` with a canned `provider_details` dict mirroring the actual pydantic-ai OpenAI provider output, asserting the detector fires — then verify the dict structure matches what the live library produces.

findings.finding: The body explicitly states 'this story BUMPS the pin to `json-repair>=0.61`' as a prerequisite for the schema-guided repair argument, but no acceptance criterion verifies the version pin change itself. AC3 checks that `schema=<model>` is called, but if the pin is not bumped the call would fail at runtime in environments with json-repair 0.30–0.60 — the pin bump is load-bearing work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Body: 'BUMPS the pin to json-repair>=0.61'; Acceptance Criteria section; pyproject.toml
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'pyproject.toml pins json-repair>=0.61; if the pin cannot be satisfied in a consumer environment, the try/except fallback ensures >=0.30 continues to work schema-lessly (expand-contract: the code fallback is the rollback path for the pin change).'

findings.finding: The plan's `json-repair` schema-guided repair claim — '`repair_json` params include `schema` + `schema_repair_mode` (VERIFIED offline, json-repair 0.61.2)' — cannot be grounded from any repo artifact. The `pyproject.toml` pins `json-repair>=0.30` (confirmed), and the plan proposes bumping to `>=0.61`. But the 'verification' evidence exists only as prose in the plan's own 'Verified library behavior' block; there is no lockfile, no import-introspect fixture, no test exercising `schema=` or `schema_repair_mode`, and no pinned version in the repo that confirms 0.61.2 is installed.
findings.location: What section — 'Output self-heal in structured.py' item (1); 'Verified library behavior' block
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_structured.py` or a new `test_json_repair_schema.py`, add a parametrized test that calls `repair_json(malformed_json, return_objects=True, schema=SomeModel.model_json_schema(), schema_repair_mode='strict')` and asserts the result matches the schema — this directly validates both the param existence and the floor bump.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `repair_json` accepts `schema=<model>` and `schema_repair_mode='strict'` parameters, claiming 'VERIFIED offline (json-repair 0.61.2)', but no observable artifact supports this claim: there is no captured --help output, no doc quote, no code citation from json-repair's source, and no test in the repo that exercises these parameters. The only repo usage is `repair_json(cand, return_objects=True)` (structured.py:161), and the current pin is `json-repair>=0.30` — a version where these params may be absent. AC#3 depends directly on this capability.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal, item (1): 'add `schema=<model-json-schema>, schema_repair_mode=\'strict\'`'; AC#3: '`json_repair` is called with `schema=<model>`'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line verification test (or embed it in the existing `test_structured.py` parametrize table): `from json_repair import repair_json; import inspect; assert 'schema' in inspect.signature(repair_json).parameters`, gated on the bumped pin. This converts the unverified claim to an observed, CI-enforced fact.

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema), so the consumer interface is present; AC confirms the installed version accepts it' — but there is no in-repo experiment log, import probe, unit test, or changelog reference grounding that the installed json-repair>=0.30 version actually supports the schema= and schema_repair_mode parameters. The string 'schema_repair_mode' appears nowhere in the repo. If this parameter was introduced in a later release than >=0.30 covers, or was introduced under a different name, AC item 3 ('json_repair is called with schema=<model>') will silently fail at runtime.
findings.location: What section — Output self-heal bullet (1): 'The pinned json-repair exposes schema= + schema_repair_mode (verified: repair_json params include schema)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that 'story mamba' in the 'Routing mechanism' paragraph refers to `rebar.llm.failure` (i.e., `from rebar.llm.failure import LLMOutcome, sanitize_diagnostic, ResolutionClass`) so the implementation target is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan refers to the shared `LLMOutcome`/`sanitize_diagnostic` dependency story by two different names in two different sections: 'story mamba' (in the 'Routing mechanism' paragraph) and implicitly via `failure.py`'s own docstring 'story civilized-immediate-mamba'. The 'What' section does not mention '30ff'; however the plan-cache files show a prior finding flagged '30ff' vs 'mamba' inconsistency. This is minor but the plan body as submitted uses only 'mamba' and 'story mamba' consistently, so no real ambiguity remains — the symbol `LLMOutcome` from `failure.py` is the right target.
findings.location: What section — 'Routing mechanism' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add the import path inline: 'the SAME `LLMOutcome` type from `rebar.llm.failure` (story civilized-immediate-mamba)'.

findings.finding: The Anthropic refusal-as-content detection condition is underspecified: the plan says `finish_reason` normalizes to `content_filter` for Anthropic AND `provider_details` 'may carry' the raw stop_reason, but never states the concrete boolean expression an executor must implement for the Anthropic path — specifically whether the trigger is `finish_reason == 'content_filter' AND non_empty_text_parts`, or whether `provider_details` must also be inspected when `finish_reason` is normal.
findings.location: What section — Refusal-as-content protocol paragraph: 'for Anthropic (rebar's provider), getattr(result.response,\'finish_reason\',None) normalizes a refusal to content_filter and provider_details (a dict, present on ModelResponse) may carry the raw stop_reason'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line pseudocode expression to the spec, e.g.: 'Anthropic refusal-as-content fires when `finish_reason in ("content_filter",) AND any text part is non-empty; if Anthropic returns a normal finish_reason with a refusal in content, the provider_details key to inspect is `<key>`.' This disambiguates from the existing check_stop_reason path.

findings.finding: The refusal-as-content detection predicate for Anthropic is mechanistically ambiguous: the plan says finish_reason 'normalizes a refusal to content_filter' for Anthropic AND that provider_details 'may carry the raw stop_reason' — but if normalization always produces finish_reason==content_filter, check_stop_reason already catches it (content_filter is in _UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS). The new detection is only needed when finish_reason is NORMAL (e.g. stop) and the refusal signal is carried exclusively in provider_details. The plan does not state this boolean distinction explicitly, so an implementer cannot derive a correct non-overlapping predicate: they may write a check that fires on the already-handled content_filter case (a no-op duplicate) or miss the provider_details-only case (the actual #5221 gap) entirely.
findings.location: What section — 'Refusal-as-content protocol (concrete, distinct from check_stop_reason)' paragraph; runner.py:497-502 (NativeOutput) and runner.py:519 (PromptedOutput)
findings.suggested_fix: In the detection rule, write: 'The refusal-as-content check is a SECOND guard applied only when `finish_reason` is not in `_UNRETRYABLE_STOP_REASONS` (i.e. `check_stop_reason` passed). It then inspects `provider_details`: for Anthropic, `provider_details.get("stop_reason") in {"refusal", "content_filter"}`; for OpenAI, `provider_details.get("incomplete_details", {}).get("reason") == "content_filter"`. Both require `finish_reason` to be otherwise-normal — that is the precise additive gap over the existing check.'

findings.finding: The refusal-as-content check is described as being added only to the NativeOutput branch, but rebar's current provider (Anthropic) uses the PromptedOutput branch — meaning the primary live code path for the current provider would remain unprotected against the refusal-as-content failure mode (#5221) that motivates this story.
findings.location: What section: 'Runtime result-validation on BOTH structured output paths' and 'this story adds the same check_stop_reason(...) + refusal-as-content check on the NativeOutput branch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section: 'The refusal-as-content check is added to BOTH branches: NativeOutput (new) and PromptedOutput (augmenting the existing check_stop_reason call there, which currently handles only SDK-signaled refusals with empty parts, not non-empty content with out-of-band filter signals).'

findings.finding: The plan title and 'Why' section list wall-clock timeout as one of this story's three silent-success detection mechanisms, but the body delegates it entirely to story hoopoe ('The per-request/tool timeout from story hoopoe is the silent-hang backstop'). AC5 ('RED silent-success cases flip GREEN') does not carve out the hang case, so the AC would be unverifiable for silent hangs if hoopoe has not yet landed — a cross-section contradiction between the stated scope and the verifiable acceptance criteria.
findings.location: Plan title / 'Why' section vs body ('per-request/tool timeout from story hoopoe') vs AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan title change 'wall-clock timeout + verdict schema-shape + structured refusal-as-content' to 'verdict schema-shape + structured refusal-as-content (wall-clock timeout via story hoopoe)'; add a parenthetical to AC5: 'RED silent-success cases for refusal-as-content and truncation flip GREEN; silent-hang coverage is contingent on story hoopoe landing first.'

findings.finding: The AC that says 'RED silent-success cases flip GREEN' (AC5) and 'a test asserts the detector does NOT false-fire on a normal verdict' (AC2 proxy) do not name any test file paths, test function names, or `pytest -k` expressions, so a verifier cannot confirm that the RED baseline is fully covered or that the false-positive test was actually written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 ('RED silent-success cases flip GREEN; make test/make lint/make typecheck green') and 'A test asserts the detector does NOT false-fire...' in the Refusal-as-content protocol paragraph

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
